title: Ethics in psychological research
summary: Psychology can only study people if it protects them first. This lesson builds the full set of ethical considerations for research with human participants — informed consent, deception, the right to withdraw, protection from harm, confidentiality and anonymity, and debriefing — then extends to animal research (the 3Rs) and shows how ethical constraints actually shape research design, the conclusions we can draw, and the validity of a study. Throughout, classic studies are used to illustrate ethical issues without endorsing them.
learningObjectives: Explain the key ethical considerations in research with human participants: informed consent, deception, the right to withdraw, protection from harm, confidentiality/anonymity and debriefing.
Explain the ethical framework for animal research — the 3Rs (replacement, reduction, refinement) — and how animal studies are justified.
Analyse how ethical constraints affect research design and the conclusions and validity of a study.
Identify the ethical issues in a described study and use classic studies to illustrate ethical problems without endorsing them.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: The Psychologist's Duty of Care
simpleExplanation.summary: Ethical guidelines are the rules that keep the pursuit of knowledge from harming the people (or animals) who make that knowledge possible. They protect participants — and, in doing so, also protect the credibility of the science.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of a doctor running a health check. Before anything happens they explain what they will do and why (informed consent), they don't secretly test something you never agreed to (avoiding deception), they let you stop at any moment (right to withdraw), they make sure the procedure won't hurt you (protection from harm), they keep your results private (confidentiality), and afterwards they tell you what they found and what it means (debriefing). A researcher owes participants exactly this same duty of care.
steps: Scarcity of trust: people will only take part if they are respected, so ethics is the foundation of good research, not an add-on.
Six core considerations with human participants: informed consent, deception, right to withdraw, protection from harm, confidentiality/anonymity, debriefing.
With animals, the 3Rs — replacement, reduction, refinement — plus a clear justification for any harm.
Ethical limits shape what can be studied and how, which in turn shapes the conclusions and the validity of the findings.

flashcards.back: Participants must agree to take part after being told the nature and purpose of the study and any risks, so their agreement is genuinely voluntary and informed. Special safeguards apply for children and vulnerable groups (e.g. parental/guardian consent).
Deliberately misleading participants or withholding information about the true aim of the study. It may be by commission (actively lying) or omission (leaving key information out).

Only when it is necessary to obtain valid data (the aim could not be studied without it), the potential harm is minimal, no acceptable alternative exists, and participants are fully debriefed afterwards. Deception is a last resort, not a convenience.
Participants may leave the study at any time, for any reason, without penalty — and may also withdraw their data after taking part. This must be made clear at the start and respected throughout.

Researchers must protect participants from physical AND psychological harm (stress, embarrassment, anxiety), keeping any risk no greater than everyday life. Harm must be justified, minimised, and reversed where possible.

Confidentiality: the researcher knows who the participant is but does not disclose their identity (data are coded). Anonymity: the researcher cannot link data to any individual at all (e.g. an unnamed online survey). Anonymity is stricter.

A post-study explanation of the true aim, revealing any deception, checking the participant's wellbeing, offering support, and reminding them of their right to withdraw their data. It aims to return participants to the state they were in before the study.

Replacement — use non-animal methods where possible; Reduction — use the fewest animals needed for valid results; Refinement — adjust procedures to minimise suffering. Any remaining harm must be justified by the scientific value of the research.

The judgement an ethics committee makes when weighing the potential costs to participants (stress, deception, harm) against the potential scientific and social benefits. Research is only approved if benefits clearly outweigh costs.

An alternative used when full prior consent would ruin validity: a similar group is asked whether they would agree to the procedure, and their agreement is taken to indicate the real participants would consent too. It does not fully replace informed consent.
Listing 'deception, consent, harm' scores little. Each ethical consideration must be defined AND tied to a specific detail of the study, explaining WHY it is an issue there.

Ethical limits shape design — e.g. debriefing, obtaining consent, or avoiding harm can change how participants behave (demand characteristics) or restrict which situations can be studied, which in turn limits the conclusions and can raise or lower internal and ecological validity.

quickCheck.prompt: A researcher runs a study on obedience but tells participants it is a study 'about learning'. This is best described as:
options: A violation of the right to withdraw

quickCheck.prompt: A participant becomes distressed halfway through a study and asks to stop, but the researcher insists they finish 'because the data will be useless otherwise'. Which principle has been violated?
What is the PRIMARY purpose of debriefing?
options: To collect extra data after the study
To explain the true aim, reverse any harm or deception, and check the participant's wellbeing
To obtain consent before the study begins
To keep the participant's identity secret
quickCheck.answer: To explain the true aim, reverse any harm or deception, and check the participant's wellbeing

quickCheck.prompt: In the 3Rs framework for animal research, 'Refinement' means:
options: Using fewer animals in the study
Replacing animals with computer models where possible
Adjusting procedures to minimise the animals' suffering
Repeating the study to confirm the results
quickCheck.answer: Adjusting procedures to minimise the animals' suffering

quickCheck.prompt: A researcher knows each participant's name but stores their data under a code and never publishes their identities. This is:

faq.q: What is the difference between confidentiality and anonymity?
faq.a: Anonymity means the researcher cannot link the data to any specific person — for example an online survey that collects no names or IP addresses. Confidentiality means the researcher does know who the participant is but promises not to disclose their identity, usually by storing data under a code. Most psychological research is confidential rather than truly anonymous.

faq.q: Is it ever acceptable to use deception?
faq.a: Yes, but only under strict conditions: the deception must be necessary because the study's aim could not otherwise be investigated validly, the potential harm must be minimal, there must be no acceptable non-deceptive alternative, and participants must be fully debriefed as soon as possible so they can withdraw their data if they wish. Deception is a justified last resort, not a shortcut.

faq.q: How do ethical constraints affect the validity of research?
faq.a: Ethical requirements shape the design, and design shapes validity. Fully informing participants of the aim can create demand characteristics that lower internal validity; avoiding harm may force a study into an artificial lab setting that lowers ecological validity; the need for consent may make some real-world situations impossible to study at all. So ethics does not just protect people — it partly determines what conclusions a study can legitimately support.

faq.q: Why are classic ethically-controversial studies still taught?
faq.a: Because they illustrate ethical issues vividly and shaped the guidelines we now use. The point is to analyse and evaluate them — to explain what went wrong and how modern ethics would prevent it — not to endorse or recommend repeating them. In an exam, use them as examples of issues, and be explicit that they would not be approved today.

sections.content: Psychology aims to understand the mind and behaviour, but it can only do so by studying real people and animals. That creates a tension: the pursuit of knowledge must never override the welfare, dignity and rights of participants. This lesson builds the full set of ethical considerations for human research, extends them to animal research through the 3Rs, and shows how these constraints shape not only what is allowed but the conclusions and validity of a study — a skill Paper 3 rewards directly.
Why ethics sits at the centre of psychological research

Ethical guidelines are not bureaucratic hurdles; they are the foundation of responsible science. They developed largely in response to earlier research where participants were harmed or misled. Professional bodies such as the British Psychological Society (BPS) and the American Psychological Association (APA) publish codes of conduct, and studies are screened by an ethics committee (or Institutional Review Board) that weighs the costs to participants against the potential benefits of the research. For the exam you need to explain each consideration AND apply it to a specific study.

items: **Informed consent** — participants must agree to take part after being told the nature and purpose of the study and any risks, so their agreement is voluntary and informed. Children and vulnerable groups need additional safeguards (e.g. parental consent).

**Deception** — deliberately misleading participants or withholding key information. It should be avoided, but where it is essential to validity it must be minimal, justified, and always followed by a debrief.
**Right to withdraw** — participants may leave at any time without penalty and may withdraw their data afterwards; this must be stated clearly and respected throughout.
**Protection from harm** — participants must be protected from physical and psychological harm (stress, anxiety, embarrassment), with risk kept no greater than everyday life.
**Confidentiality and anonymity** — identifying data must be kept private; confidentiality protects a known identity, anonymity means the researcher cannot identify the participant at all.
**Debriefing** — after the study, participants are told the true aim, any deception is revealed, their wellbeing is checked, and support is offered so they leave in the state they arrived in.

sections.content: Deception deserves special care because it directly conflicts with informed consent: if participants are deceived, their prior consent was not fully informed. The justification test is strict — deception is acceptable only when the aim genuinely could not be studied without it, the harm is minimal, no acceptable alternative exists, and a thorough debrief follows. This is where presumptive consent (asking a similar group whether they would agree) and a robust debrief become the researcher's tools for keeping a deceptive design ethical.

sections.question:
A researcher investigates stress and memory. Volunteers are told only that they are taking part in a 'general memory task'. Each is unexpectedly asked to give a five-minute speech in front of a panel who frown and interrupt, then complete a recall test. Several participants become visibly anxious; one asks to stop but is told 'please continue, we are nearly finished'. Afterwards participants are thanked and sent home with no further explanation, and their names are printed alongside their recall scores in the published report.

**Identify and explain three ethical considerations raised by this study.** (Use specific details from the scenario.)
sections.solution:
**1. Deception / lack of informed consent.** Participants were told only that it was a 'general memory task' and were not warned about the stressful public-speaking panel. Because the true stressful nature of the procedure was withheld, their consent was not fully informed — they agreed to something different from what actually happened.

**2. Protection from harm (psychological) and the right to withdraw.** The frowning, interrupting panel caused visible anxiety, which is psychological harm exceeding everyday life. When one participant asked to stop, the researcher pressured them to continue ('we are nearly finished'), directly violating the right to withdraw without penalty.

**3. Confidentiality and failure to debrief.** Publishing participants' names next to their recall scores breaches confidentiality by exposing identifiable data. Sending participants home 'with no further explanation' means there was no debrief — the deception was never revealed, wellbeing was not checked, and the harm was not addressed.

(Each of these is a distinct, correctly explained consideration tied to a specific detail — the structure our marking engine rewards.)

sections.content: For 'identify and explain' questions, never just name the principle. Use a three-move structure for each point: (1) name the consideration, (2) quote or paraphrase the exact detail in the study that triggers it, (3) explain WHY that detail is an ethical problem. Vague answers like 'they should be debriefed' score less than 'the researcher must reveal the true stressful aim and check for lasting anxiety'.

Some psychological research uses animals — for example to study the brain, learning or the effects of deprivation in ways that would be impossible or unethical with humans. Because animals cannot consent, a different framework applies. Researchers must satisfy the 3Rs and justify any remaining harm through a cost–benefit judgement: the scientific and social value of the findings must outweigh the suffering caused.

items: **Replacement** — wherever possible, replace animals with alternatives such as computer models, cell cultures, or human studies.
**Reduction** — use the smallest number of animals that still yields valid, reliable results, for example through efficient design and statistics.
**Refinement** — adjust housing and procedures to minimise pain, stress and lasting harm (better anaesthesia, enrichment, humane endpoints).
**Justification** — any harm that remains must be justified by the importance of the research and approved by an ethics committee; suffering with no clear scientific value is not permitted.

sections.content: How ethical constraints shape research design and validity

Ethics is not only about protecting participants — it also constrains what researchers can do, and therefore what they can conclude. This is a higher-level point examiners reward. Requiring full informed consent can introduce demand characteristics: if participants know the exact aim, they may behave differently, lowering internal validity. Avoiding psychological harm can push a study into a tightly controlled but artificial lab, lowering ecological validity. And some real-world phenomena (extreme stress, genuine emergencies, coercion) simply cannot be recreated ethically, so they can only be studied through natural experiments, case studies or correlational methods, each with its own limits on causal conclusions.

The key evaluative move is to link the ethical constraint to the conclusion: because a design had to be modified for ethical reasons, the findings may generalise less well, or the cause–effect claim may be weaker. Strong Paper 3 answers show this two-way relationship — ethics shapes design, and design shapes the validity of the conclusions.
Using classic studies to illustrate ethical issues (without endorsing them)

Several well-known studies are famous partly because of the ethical problems they raised — for example research on obedience that deceived participants and exposed them to severe stress, or simulation studies that caused psychological harm and blurred the researcher's duty of care. These are useful examples precisely because they show what can go wrong: inadequate protection from harm, deception without sufficient justification, and difficulty exercising the right to withdraw. The exam skill is to analyse them — to explain which principles were breached and how modern guidelines would prevent a repeat — not to defend or recommend them. Make it explicit in your answer that such studies would not gain ethical approval today.

sections.question: Paper 3 style: **Explain two ethical considerations that psychologists must address when conducting research with human participants. [4]**
sections.solution:
**Model answer:**
One ethical consideration is *informed consent*. Before taking part, participants must be told the nature and purpose of the study and any risks involved, and must then agree voluntarily. This matters because participation should be a free, informed choice rather than something people are tricked or pressured into; where participants are children or otherwise unable to consent, a parent or guardian must consent on their behalf.

A second consideration is *protection from harm*. Researchers must ensure participants suffer no lasting physical or psychological damage, keeping any stress, anxiety or discomfort no greater than they would meet in everyday life. If some distress is unavoidable, it must be minimised, justified, and addressed afterwards — for example through debriefing and offers of support — so that participants leave the study in the state they entered it.

---
**How our marking engine awards the marks (2 considerations × 2 marks = [4]):**
The engine uses analytic points marking — each distinct, valid point scores 1 mark:
- **Informed consent — 1 mark** for identifying it and 1 mark for a correct explanation, e.g. 'participants must agree knowing the nature/purpose of the study, so their agreement is voluntary and informed'.
- **Protection from harm — 1 mark** for identifying it and 1 mark for a correct explanation, e.g. 'participants must suffer no lasting physical or psychological damage, with risk kept to everyday levels'.
- The mark for identification (**A**) is awarded only when the consideration is *correctly explained*, not merely named — 'deception' listed with no explanation scores 0 for that point.
- **ECF** (error carried forward): if a student mislabels a consideration but explains the correct idea accurately, the explanation marks can still be awarded.
- **Accept equivalents**: any two valid considerations count (e.g. right to withdraw, confidentiality/anonymity, deception, debriefing), so long as each is a *distinct* point — two rewordings of the same idea score once, not twice.

sections.content: This is exactly what the Practice button does: write your own answer to this [4]-mark question, submit it, and our marking engine grades it point by point against the same scheme shown above — telling you which considerations earned a mark and where an explanation was missing. The habit that gains marks: give TWO genuinely distinct considerations and EXPLAIN each, rather than listing four names.

items: **Naming instead of explaining** — listing 'consent, deception, harm' with no explanation or link to the study scores almost nothing; each point must be defined and applied.

**Confusing informed consent with deception** — they are related but distinct: deception is actively misleading participants, while lack of informed consent is failing to tell them what taking part involves. Explain the specific one the scenario shows.

**Treating the right to withdraw as only 'before the study'** — it applies during the study AND afterwards (withdrawing data), and pressuring someone to continue is a breach even if they technically 'could' leave.
**Thinking debriefing cancels all harm** — a debrief helps reverse deception and check wellbeing, but it does not automatically justify serious harm; the study must be defensible in the first place.

**Mixing up confidentiality and anonymity** — confidentiality protects a known identity; anonymity means the researcher cannot identify the participant at all. Using the wrong term loses precision marks.
**Muddling the 3Rs** — Replacement (use non-animal alternatives), Reduction (fewer animals), Refinement (less suffering) are distinct; swapping their meanings is penalised.
**Endorsing controversial classic studies** — use them to illustrate issues, but state clearly they breached ethics and would not be approved today; never present them as models to copy.

sections.label: Get a Paper 3 answer marked: explain two ethical considerations in research with human participants

items.label: British Psychological Society (BPS) Code of Ethics and Conduct
